data.title: BlackBerry Q2 Earnings Preview: Valuation And Growth Considerations
data.description:
BlackBerry Overview

On Thursday, September 25, 2025, BlackBerry (NYSE:BB) will post its second-quarter fiscal year results. Skeptical investors who abandoned BB stock might cite the company’s exit from smartphone markets. It ceded its QWERTY keyboard phone to Apple (AAPL) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF).

Since its Q1 report coverage, BB stock returned 0.5%, compared to an 11.2% return on the S&P 500 (SPY) index.

The surprising return of a nostalgic typing experience does not appear to have BlackBerry’s involvement. After the stock extended seven sessions of gains, following a German certification for UEM deployment, what should shareholders expect from BlackBerry’s quarterly earnings report?

Q2 Earnings Grade

On the BB stock page, from the earnings tab, BlackBerry has a revisions grade of “C.” Two analysts raised their revisions in the last 90 days. The earnings per share GAAP estimate is one cent on revenue of $122.03 million.

The firm beat expectations for four straight quarters. This might explain the stock’s recent uptrend. As a result, it earns a momentum grade of B (shown below).

BB stock has a hold rating on a score of 2.79/5.00. While the stock has a strong momentum and growth grade, it earns a D+ on valuation.

Is the premium valuation warranted?

First Quarter Earnings

In the Q1 report posted in June, BlackBerry reported a 15.5% Y/Y decline in revenue, to $121.7 million. It earned $0.02 a share. Readers may anticipate that in the upcoming report, the company will report revenue that exceeds consensus. In addition, it might revise its full-year revenue above consensus. However, the firm risks issuing a full-year non-GAAP EPS that is below expectations.

Q2 FY26

Full fiscal year FY26

Total BlackBerry revenue:

$115 - $125 million vs. consensus of $122.42M

$508 - $538 million vs. consensus of $513.05M

Non-GAAP basic EPS:

Breakeven - $0.01 vs. consensus of $0.01

$0.08 - $0.10 vs. consensus of $0.10

Look for QNX royalty revenue to have an impact on meeting estimates. On the conference call, BlackBerry assumed $55 million to $60 million. This estimate included risks related to disruptions and the macro environment.

QNX’s Software Development Platform ( SDP 8.0) had an $865 million backlog. Just as Oracle (ORCL) touted its remaining performance obligations or RPO, BlackBerry may do the same in the upcoming conference call. Chief Executive Officer Giamatteo said that SDP 8.0 is one of the hottest products. Demand will likely increase from the automotive sector, thanks to next-generation application use cases.

The CEO said that the product has potential markets in QNX Hypervisor (or GEM), robotics, and industrial automation.

The company has a ~ $100 million share repurchase plan. However, the negative cash flow suggests that it cannot aggressively buy back stock. Investors should not expect this initiative to support the share price. It will need to grow QNX first before BB stock forms a meaningful uptrend.

Opportunity

Markets overlook the value of BlackBerry’s secure communications offering. Geopolitical dynamics will continue to increase demand for mission-critical capabilities for governments. Instead of relying on insecure cloud infrastructure, they will require greater data privacy for their communications needs.

This bullish view requires skepticism. BlackBerry bought Cylance in 2018 for$1.4 billion. Last year, it sold the security software firm to Arctic Wolf for $160 million. Still, the CEO said that secure communications is a new value proposition in its mission-critical software. He said that people think of the company “not about necessarily that device and that keyboard, but that secure experience that you always felt.”

BlackBerry needs to leverage that brand awareness to grow IoT and QNX sales.

On Sept. 18, BlackBerry achieved BSI certification for mobile device security. Secusmart, which provides encrypted voice, data, text, and video in government, is a must-use alternative to Meta Platforms’ (META) WhatsApp. Unfortunately, WhatsApp and Signal have a bigger market share. The firm would need to consider integrating with other platforms to grow its user base.

SDP 8.0 is a growth opportunity. It is scalable, where it works on 2-3 cores but may scale to up to 64 cores. Automotive firms may vary the chip processing power for the software.

Rating: Accumulate BlackBerry Stock

BlackBerry has not posted any news since its last quarterly earnings report that would warrant a change in its stock rating. The Seeking Alpha stock grades warn on valuation risks in return for a company demonstrating steady relative growth.

The relative grade is within the systems software sector, where investors may consider Appian (APPN), Teradata (TDC), or Progress Software (PRGS). Investors may extend their worry about valuation to the broader market. Still, readers cannot worry about market-related volatility. This may change at any time.

The company’s fundamentals depend on ongoing revenue from intellectual property. Accelerated growth in QNX software is pivotal to the bullish rating in BB stock.

Above: peer comparison of BB stock.

Risks

Demand for BlackBerry’s automotive software depends on the electric vehicle market. As the$7,500 tax credit expires, Tesla (TSLA) leads in the global decline in sales. Fortunately, Chinese firms like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), XPeng (XPEV), and Nio (NIO) might take Tesla’s market share. Lower EV prices from Chinese EVs would benefit BlackBerry’s automotive software growth.

Your Takeaway

BlackBerry shares are up by 11.4% year-to-date, compared to the 12.8% return on the S&P 500 (IVV). Executives will need to reaffirm their full-year guidance to justify the stock’s monthly 15.4% return. A guidance hike might help the stock rise further from here. Conversely, the stock could drop if the guidance does not increase. This happens when markets price in the good news ahead of the earnings report.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nvidia And Intel: More Than Just Strategic Messaging, Why Both Need It
data.description:
Intel-Nvidia: A Strategic Realignment To Outmaneuver Common Rivals

Wow, wow! Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) and Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) just did what? Well, I'm not going to bore with another repeat of yesterday's headlines on Nvidia's decision to strike what CEO Jensen Huang highlighted as an incredible deal for the company. While the $5B deal (at $23.28 per common share) on surface doesn't seem much as compared to Intel's upgraded market cap of $116B, the deal immediately elevates Nvidia to becoming one of INTC's top ten shareholders.

Hence, in a short span over just over two months, INTC stock has gained from a low of under $19 through yesterday's close of $30.6 (>60% upside). Whether we agree with the upgraded buying sentiments or not, the transformation under the stewardship of CEO Lip-Bu Tan appears to be coming into fruition, as he seeks to remake the company's fortunes to compete more effectively with his semiconductor rivals. Nevertheless, Jensen was quick to downplay speculation on any US government involvement, underscoring the strategic alignment and benefits accruing to Intel and Nvidia, respectively.

As a quick reminder, Nvidia will be expected to play an even more critical role in reviving Intel's flailing products business on both the data center and the PC side of the house. Arm-based CPUs (ARM) have already been making steady gains against Intel's x86 architecture, calling into question whether INTC's long-term architecture dominance could face risks of being disrupted. And AMD (AMD) has also been making incremental gains on the PC side of the business, while already being a prominent player in data center CPUs, growing faster than Intel in deals with the hyperscalers, presaging future enterprise foothold gains.

Intel Gains Nvidia's Unrivalled Expertise. And Nvidia Gains A Deeper Foothold Into Intel's Market

With this deal, Jensen Huang believes it should open up an additional $50B in TAM for both companies in the data center and PC business, as they work more closely together in co-designing advanced and improved Intel-Nvidia chips for the market. Accordingly, we should now anticipate deeper forays with INTC on both its x86 server CPUs, while integrating Nvidia RTX GPU chiplets into Intel-based PC platforms.

Given Intel's already dominant market scale, I think this deal is arguably more than just strategic messaging if executed well. It has the potential not only to thwart the current gains made by AMD on both sides of the house (data center and PC), it could also help Nvidia diversify from being locked into Arm-based designs, lowering its concentration risks. While the partnership may not yield near-term financial benefits for now, I believe the surge in INTC shares yesterday demonstrates the market's optimism that with Nvidia becoming a one of the top shareholders, it has the potential to revive Intel's badly damaged fortunes, while also keeping Nvidia at the forefront of its primacy in AI chips.

Also, I think the crux of the optimism lies well beyond just the products side of the house, even though it may not be palpable for now. Note that the deal doesn't indicate any exclusivity between Intel and Nvidia on the x86 blueprint, notwithstanding the market optimism. And, while the current deal focuses on the design/products side of the business, could it also reignite Intel's future business opportunities with Intel foundry with the 14A roadmap?

There's no doubt that while yesterday's press conference hasn't unveiled meaningful insights on whether Nvidia could team up with Intel on future foundry partnerships, it has paved the way for such a plausibility. But, the issue is that the market seems to have driven INTC's valuation too high beyond what I think is reasonable. We need to be cautious about projecting significant confidence for now, as the partnership corresponds to what appears to be a 12–18 months medium-term roadmap (taking into account validation and testing, etc.) before we can even think about the positive impact on Intel or Nvidia's P&L. In other words, still too early to ascertain. Let's not try and overstate the optimism.

Intel: The Worst Is Arguably Over

Despite that, I think it's helpful to point out that INTC's estimates already appear to be bottoming out through the rest of 2025. Therefore, the optimism is likely predicated on more confidence in Tan's execution prowess, beyond the near-term maneuvers to recast the company's overloaded bureaucracy and improve its cost efficiencies should also improve its battered free cash flow profitability. However, the market's skepticism on Intel's long-term data center and PC competitiveness have remained uncertain, compounding the cash burn suffered by its efforts in foundry. Hence, Nvidia's investment isn't merely about the financial injection in stabilizing Intel's balance sheet further, but also giving Tan and his team a credible shot at reviving its competitiveness against AMD.

Nvidia: Accelerating Its Push To Drive TAM Prospects

For Nvidia, while the deal appears to have been in the works for a while, the impetus to explore new material growth vectors have become increasingly important, given the snafu facing its China business (incidentally also a $50B TAM opportunity).

We already know that Broadcom (AVGO) is making a sustained push to take market share with the hyperscalers based on its more cost-efficient custom chips. And as inferencing workloads become potentially even more dominant moving forward, Nvidia needs to ramp up its efforts to embed itself deeper within the semiconductor value chain to maintain its primacy.

Hence, I don't think it's any coincidence that Huang realized that a timely partnership with Intel serves well to promulgate the message to investors that Nvidia has ways to open up new medium- to long-term growth optionalities even if the company faces challenges to its leadership by arch-rivals. And for Intel (arguably being left behind in this AI race), the opportunity to seize a lifeline of sorts to close the gap against AMD cannot be missed.

Buy Or Sell?

Interestingly, NVDA has underperformed INTC since August, as investors arguably reallocated to the other opportunities within semis that also includes AVGO. AMD stock has also underperformed recently, corroborating my view.

Despite that, I'm still leaning in favor of NVDA from a longer-term perspective, and will look to buy the dips if we do get a steeper pullback. While Broadcom appears to wield a more substantive influence with its gains through custom chips and high speed networking prospects, Jensen Huang has proved time and time again that he has "new tricks" up his sleeves to enthrall his loyal followers and investors.

On the other hand, INTC's surge yesterday has pushed the stock up to a valuation of 12.1x forward EBITDA, in line with the +2 standard deviation zone over its 10Y average. In other words, if you didn't load up before the surge, I don't think the current opportunity is timely.

Hence, while both stocks are rated as HOLD for now, NVDA's long-term uptrend over INTC validates my confidence. Moreover, such pullbacks aren't unique, as seen with the downward arrows annotated in my chart above.

Rating: Maintain Hold on NVDA. Maintain Hold on INTC.

data.title: Uber Has Lyft To Worry About: Maintaining Both With A Buy
data.description:
Lyft (NASDAQ:LYFT) took over the headlines on Wednesday as the stock surged as much as 14% in one trading day. These kinds of moves have been unusual for Lyft, but the company is aligning itself with the big boys now, giving investors a front-row seat to the robotaxi revolution. So, what happened? Google’s (GOOG) Waymo has been partnering with Uber (NYSE:UBER) for years now, already pushing aggressively beyond its core bases in San Francisco, Los Angeles, Phoenix, and Austin, now handling more than 300,000 rides per week with a fleet of 1,500 autonomous vehicles, set to grow to 2,000 by 2026. Expansion plans include new rollouts in Atlanta, Denver, and Seattle, active testing in New York City, and even early international pilots in Tokyo, with plans to launch commercial operations in Miami, Washington, D.C., Dallas, and Denver next year. Now, Lyft is part of the conversation with Waymo, with a commercial deal to enter Nashville next year.

As part of the deal, Lyft will oversee operations for Waymo’s robotaxi fleet, which is expected to scale to several hundred vehicles over time. Lyft will also “provide end-to-end fleet management services including vehicle readiness and maintenance, infrastructure, and depot operations in Nashville.” Initially, rides will be booked exclusively through Waymo’s app, but by next year, customers will be able to hail autonomous rides directly through Lyft’s platform as well. On the partnership, Lyft CEO David Risher called it a blend of “best-in-class autonomous technology with best-in-class customer experience… we actually see those markets growing faster, faster than a lot of the other markets we operate in.”

This isn’t Lyft’s first rodeo in driverless taxis, though. Earlier this month, Lyft launched a pilot program with May Mobility in Atlanta. Globally speaking, Waymo’s biggest competitor is Baidu’s Apollo Go, which announced in August that it will team up with Lyft to roll out driverless taxis in Europe next year. Just a month prior, Baidu also revealed a partnership with Uber to deploy autonomous cars outside the U.S. and mainland China, starting in Asia and the Middle East later this year.

Lyft and Waymo have some history, having run a pilot together in Phoenix back in 2019. However, Waymo chose Uber in Phoenix, and it currently operates directly through its own app. Which brings me to my next point: Uber and Lyft have long been pitted against each other, and this AV push, in my opinion, gives them a fighting chance. With Uber’s market cap around 25x that of Lyft’s, the latter has its work cut out for it. Still, this partnership feels like a strategic turning point. For years, Lyft has been viewed as the perpetual underdog, a company that could deliver steady ride growth but struggled to differentiate itself from Uber’s scale.

The company is re-entering the growth conversation, with AVs acting as a margin lever rather than a cash drain, if the company can execute well. Of course, there are risks. Waymo may still prioritize Uber in its larger rollouts, but for Lyft, the Nashville deal provides proof of relevance at a time when investors had largely written off its ability to compete, with the stock down 71.95% from its all-time levels, reflecting a long decline since its 2019 IPO near $78. Over the years, Lyft peaked around the $65–70 range in 2020–2021 before trending downward, bottoming below $10 in 2023–2024.

Investment thesis for Uber and Lyft:

While Uber remains the dominant player, Lyft finally has a narrative that goes beyond survival. Thus, I’m maintaining both with a buy, but see more upside ahead for Uber as the company’s long-term leadership in the robotaxi race looks more entrenched, even against Tesla (TSLA), and I discuss this further in my “Alphabet's Waymo Is Winning, And Tesla Is Playing Catch-Up” article.

So yes, while Lyft’s partnership is a proof-of-concept, Uber’s is a full-fledged growth engine. Both benefit from Waymo’s success, but Uber remains the more direct and scaled play on the commercialization of autonomous ride-hailing. The market recognizes that, and Lyft’s move on Thursday, down around 4%, shows the positives priced in, for now.

While both stocks are outperforming the S&P500’s 13% run year to date, Lyft is up a staggering 70%, while Uber is up a more conservative 57% during the same period. I last covered Uber in May of this year, and the stock is up around 13% since, underperforming the S&P500, up 17.7%. I also recommended Uber with a buy back during the February low, and the stock is up around 35% since.

I saw Uber’s move into lower-density, rural markets as the catalyst that could lift mobility into a more profitable growth trajectory, and autonomous vehicles poised to give Uber’s mobility segment a clear competitive advantage over Lyft in the coming year. Now both have a seat at the table, but Uber’s first-mover advantage gives it an edge. Uber isn't solely a ride-hailing company anymore, as the company’s delivery segment is also gaining some traction, through its latest announcement to launch drone deliveries via Uber Eats through a partnership with Flytrex (which holds FAA approval for Beyond Visual Line of Sight operations and has completed over 200,000 deliveries). With cars, bikes, robots, and drones as part of the equation now for Uber, I think this gives it an edge against competitors in delivery times, even strengthening expansion into suburban areas, and reducing costs.

Meanwhile, I last covered Lyft in mid-February, and the stock is up around 56.7% since, outperforming the S&P500, up 8.2% during the same period, by a large margin. This is mostly backed by the Waymo partnership, but Lyft has gained some momentum leading to that, up from a low of $9 in April to $20 in mid-September.

I mentioned Lyft’s push into AVs in my last article, saying that:

As for Lyft, I see long-term positive AV partnerships as a stepping stone to bigger things...On the AV front, while I was watching the Mobility partnership last quarter to support outperformance in 2025, it's coming sooner than expected through a new partnership with Marubeni and new partnerships on the horizon. The AVs are transforming the ride-hailing marketplace, and soon enough, there will be more market share to snatch. I think Lyft is a good contender.” On its 2Q25 earnings call, Risher said “whenever you think about AV is you have to think about them as a massive TAM expander for rideshare. And the reason I'm so confident in this is we see the data. We see the data. So for example, in the markets where [indiscernible] is in the United States, in the markets where AVs are operational, and these are the San Franciscos and LAs and Phoenixes of the United States, we are seeing growth, industry growth I'm talking about, that is 5x larger, 5x larger than what you're seeing in other top markets, 5x larger.

I maintain this sentiment and continue to see more upside ahead for Lyft on the AV front, in addition to its efforts in the pricing strategy to win the price war with Uber through cool initiatives like Price Lock.

Risher talked about it some more during the call, and said that “commute is our single biggest use case, as you know, and it has got incredible retention rates, Price Lock does. So I look at it not just in terms of pricing and affordability, but also in terms of reliability and like that.” This is a further reflection of Lyft’s customer-obsession mantra.

Valuation and price movement:

On forward valuation, Lyft screens cheaper than Uber on several metrics: its P/E Non-GAAP (FWD) is 18.1 versus Uber’s 25.7, forward EV/Sales sits at 1.2 compared to Uber’s 3.9, and forward EV/EBITDA is 15.3 against Uber’s 22.8. Lyft also trades at a lower forward EV/EBIT of 19.8 versus Uber’s 32.5.

On the growth side, both companies deliver above-sector revenue expansion, with Lyft slightly ahead at 18.8% versus Uber’s 16.8%, and EBITDA growth of 43.6% versus 39.8%. However, Uber dominates when it comes to operating leverage, with EBIT growth projected at nearly 96% compared to Lyft’s 70%, and it has far clearer earnings visibility, with EPS growth near 60% while Lyft remains non-meaningful. In short, Lyft offers discounted multiples and higher long-term CAGR potential, but I think Uber justifies its premium with stronger earnings power, free cash flow visibility, and diversified growth drivers.

Uber is showing stability by holding above all EMAs, while Lyft has been on a breakout run but now looks technically overheated, with RSI at 74. In terms of momentum, Lyft carries more near-term strength, yet this leaves it vulnerable to a pullback, whereas Uber appears calmer but well-positioned for another breakout if it can clear the $100 resistance level.

What’s next?

While Uber has a premium valuation against Lyft, I think it was well deserved. Now, while the gap is narrowing, I continue to think Uber has more upside in the short-term. I say this considering that the AV positives are priced into Lyft's stock for the near term, but do give the company a much-needed edge against Uber in the long run.

data.title: Lululemon: The Best Performance, The Lowest Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

With the recent launch of Lululemon here in Brussels, I went to explore the flagship new store and saw a crowd battling each other for the newest Lululemon product releases. Now, a shop might not be indicative of the broad operation, and certainly not on a launch day, but I want to explore the potential value behind the once-shining growth story.

Lululemon Athletica (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:LULU) saw its stock price decline from an ATH of over $511 back in late 2023 to now $162, a decline of over 68% which is simply astonishing in my mind for such a profitable apparel company. With the valuation crashing back to earth, I believe that the company could be a great GARP pick.

Operation Overview and Current Dynamics

Lululemon has highlighted three priorities following the April 2022 investor event, which will play out until 2027, focusing on e-commerce, product innovation, and international expansion.

Product innovation is key as the athleisure industry is highly competitive, especially on the women’s apparel side. The sector is highly known for new trends every couple of years and LULU has done a relatively strong job at capturing and holding its market share. This has been the result of the “Science of Feel” philosophy, which puts importance on the sensory experience of the user, with a focus on the fabric and design feel. This has resulted in signature materials such as Nulu and Luon that have been commanding higher prices. Furthermore, LULU has been able to create a community-driven approach in product design that has been a key differentiator against brands such as Nike (NYSE: NKE). It takes into account feedback from athletes, yoga instructors and customers to create the perfect product lines.

Outside of the women's category, LULU has been able to grow its men's lines, which went from $953MM in sales back in 2021 to now over $2.63B for the past twelve months. The key product driving this growth has been the ABC Pant, which created a new category of versatile “On the Move” apparel. On the back of this great success, LULU has been able to enter “play categories” such as golf and tennis.

LULU has also entered the footwear category back in 2022 with a “women-first” approach, which was a bold strategic move as it was generally the inverse for other brands. LULU does not directly disclose the result of its footwear category, but the “other category” that includes it has seen strong growth since the launch, seeing sales go from $657.2MM to $1.07B. Though this puts LULU in direct competition with other athletic brands such as Nike or Adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY), that have decades of experience in the field.

Regarding its Direct-to-Consumer approach, I see it as a strength as it allows LULU to have great control over its sales and does not rely on wholesale operations. It sells its product via either company-owned physical stores or e-commerce, which leaves the company in direct control of pricing and discounting, compared to competitors that have had to sell via wholesale partners and share their margins with them. As an example, Nike tried to do the same as Lululemon and saw its operation challenged and sales decline as a result.

LULU has been expanding greatly in international markets with strong growth posted in China as well as in Europe. This is on the back of the company’s strategy to avoid a one-size-fits-all approach. LULU uses localized and carefully crafted entries to each market and I’ve seen firsthand how effective it is. Here in Brussels, LULU partnered with many highly popular gym brands and curated some events or highly visible ads on the gyms’ windows to build up hype. Furthermore, it launched yoga lessons with popular local influencers to gain increased visibility. I have to say that this is quite ingenious and certainly pushed me to go and see for myself what the shop was all about. LULU also tailors its dedicated e-commerce sites and social media presence to further integrate into local cultures and preferences.

Digging deeper, China has been a great success story for the company as it became its second-largest market behind the US. LULU has built a deep understanding of the local consumer and positioned itself with the growing middle class’s focus on health and wellness. Lululemon plans on having 220 stores in the country, up from 159 in the last reported period.

Despite all those positives, Lululemon is facing increased competition from the new hit brands such as Alo Yoga and Vuori, which have harmed the company’s US sales. If we take the biggest best seller, women’s leggings, that are actively priced over $100 per unit, many companies and even athleisure start-ups could recreate this and sell lower-priced leggings, undercutting the brand. Furthermore, it is impossible to patent specific styles of clothing, limiting the ability to prevent copycats.

Valuation

It shocks me to see how cheap Lululemon is considering its growth prospects and its great brand loyalty. LULU is trading at a P/E GAAP of 10.87, considerably lower than companies such as Nike and even lower than Under Armour (NYSE: UAA), which has been going through a rough patch. It is also trading at the lowest P/E recorded over the past 10 years at least. LULU has seen its current FCF yield increase as the stock has dropped in value and is now standing at 4.7% for the LTM. Though FCF generation has dropped due to the company ramping up its CapEx following its Power of Three x2 growth strategy, increasing the number of shops across the world, as well as sinking more cash into product research. More so, the firm has great intangible value with the perceived premium nature of the brand that has allowed LULU to command higher prices. Comparing it to a closely matched peer in terms of popularity, ON Holding (NYSE:ONON), LULU has around 10x the net income while they trade at a close market capitalization.

As the company fights off competitors such as Alo Yoga in the US, I expect LULU to rely on international sales to grow its revenue base, mainly in China and in Europe as it ramps up its presence. For the Americas, LULU already has 460 stores and faces the highest level of competition, which should slow down its largest segment. Though the international expansion might surely translate into slightly lower margins. If we compare to the past 3 years, ON has posted the greatest growth across all metrics compared to its peers on the back of its tailored and carefully crafted expansion.

Profitability-wise, Lululemon is also off the charts, with the greatest overall margins across the board, to what I believe could be linked to its whole in-house sales experience capturing better price performance. LULU is also able to be highly efficient with its stores with sales per square foot reaching nearly $1,600 in 2024. Furthermore, the apparel company has drastically increased its gross margin over the past 10 years, as it was 48.4% back in 2015, to now being over 59%. LULU has also been able to earn far more than its average cost of capital with an ROIC not being lower than 26% over the past decade, and sticking at 32.3% for now. I expect this trend to continue on this trajectory for the forseeable future, with an ROE at 42.7% and a ROCE of 41.8%.

Risks

As mentioned earlier, competition is heating up for the company with its new entry in many facets of apparel that could pressure its sales. The fashion industry is always riddled with new entrants that are set to disrupt the established references. The US market is saturated, and LULU is now dependent on regions where it has a smaller brand recognition to garner substantial revenue growth.

Like many apparel brands, Lululemon produces its products in Asia, and is subject to tariffs. The company’s revenue could be affected, especially if tariffs lead to worsening economic conditions, though I expect the company to be able to offset the added costs.

Conclusion

With all of that being said, I can not believe that a company with such strong growth prospects is trading at this low of a valuation. Even Nike and Under Armour their fair share of troubles in the past couples of years, are commanding higher valuation multiples. Lululemon has a lot of winners in terms of products, and with their R&D culture, I don't see how it will not continue in the future. I rate the company as a Strong Buy.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: IHAK: Sticking With Global Cybersecurity Stocks
data.description:
Global cybersecurity stocks generally performed in line with the iShares All-Country World ETF (ACWI) from late 2020 through 2024. It participated in both the ups and downs, but this year has been a tougher slog (on a relative basis) for the iShares Cybersecurity and Tech ETF (NYSEARCA:IHAK).

I had a "Buy" rating on IHAK back in March, and shares have done fine, up 8% on a total return basis. That’s about 7 percentage points of negative alpha to the ACWI. Last Thursday, though, cybersecurity equities got a boost courtesy of perhaps the strongest player in the pack.

CrowdStrike (CRWD) shares rose following solid guidance offered at its Analyst Day. It now expects annual recurring revenue growth of at least 20% through 2027, amid global investment in digital defenses. It reports earnings next on Nov. 25.

For now, I keep a "Buy" rating on IHAK. I think a technical breakout could happen while the valuation is at a deserved premium to the global stock market.

IHAK: Losing Some Ground vs. the ACWI ETF

According to the issuer, IHAK "seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of developed and emerging market companies involved in cybersecurity and technology, including cybersecurity hardware, software, products, and services." It offers investors exposure to companies at the forefront of cybersecurity innovation, both at home and abroad, primarily in the Information Technology sector.

IHAK remains a somewhat small ETF with just $940 million in assets under management as of Sept. 18, 2025. That’s up a bit from $926 million at the time of my previous analysis. The annual expense ratio is moderate at 47 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at only 0.19%.

Share-price momentum has been just OK in recent months, now rated a B- in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. On risk, the concentrated, industry-focused fund does have occasional bouts of volatility, so position sizing is key.

Finally, liquidity can be an issue at times, given the low average daily trading volume of just 56,000 shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of 10 basis points, per iShares. For that reason, I encourage prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market’s open.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar primarily holds growth and SMID-cap stocks from around the world. Just 12% of IHAK is classified as value, while only 18% of the fund is in the large-cap arena.

Thus, much hinges on the bullish story of the need for companies, big and small, to invest in technological protections. The good news from a diversification perspective is that investors get about 25% non-U.S. exposure with IHAK. That would likely help if we see more weaknesses in the value of the U.S. dollar.

On valuation, not a lot has changed from six months ago. The price-to-earnings ratio remains in the mid-20s. The long-term earnings growth rate, however, has jumped by more than 3 percentage points, which helps to bring down the PEG ratio. Thus, I keep with my "Buy" rating.

IHAK: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

IHAK is focused on the Information Technology sector, with just a sliver invested in Industrials. I’ll be particularly interested in how the upcoming earnings season plays out, in light of the bullish CrowdStrike guidance posted earlier this week. That won’t begin until the final two weeks of October.

IHAK: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, IHAK is bucking the usual trend of bearish Septembers. Up by more than 2% so far this month, we typically don’t see a rebound until October. As a result, I’m encouraged by price action.

IHAK: Usual Bearish Seasonal Trends Not Playing Out

The Technical Take

With a deserved premium valuation and decent recent returns, IHAK’s technical situation is mixed but generally constructive. Notice in the chart below that shares are close to all-time highs.

Of course, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average, the S&P 500, the Nasdaq Composite, the Russell 2000, and the All-Country World ex-US Index all notching record levels this week, you’d expect IHAK to be strong. I like that the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has been trending up with price, while a high amount of volume by price is now below the current level.

But take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s almost flat with respect to the slope, suggesting that the bulls don’t have a stronghold on the broader trend. Moreover, there’s apparent resistance in the $53 to $54 range, making the current upward thrust key for the bulls to sustain. Support, meanwhile, is likely to appear down between $49 and $50.

IHAK: Shares Rally Toward 2025 Resistance Levels

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on IHAK. I like the international diversification in today’s environment, while price action is solid along with the valuation perspective.

data.title: AWP: 12% Yield, 2% Total Returns, And 3% Premium Equals 100% Regret
data.description:
Yes, that's our equation and we are sticking to it.

We have lost the wager on how silly things would get in this market. That, of course, has not changed our mission to keep pointing out the risks with ownership of poor stocks, ETFs and CEFs. Today we tackle abrdn Global Premier Property (NYSE:AWP). AWP started at the unfortunate point just before the global financial crisis with a starting IPO price of (checks notes) $20.00 per share.

So when you see the $4.00 price, keep that in mind.

The Fund

The fund has only a modest $385 million of total assets and right there in the beginning, we can see perhaps the only thing we like about the fund. AWP uses very modest levels of leverage. In a world gone mad with levering up, AWP has got at least these ducks in a row. Unfortunately the very next part of the picture immediately shows you the big problem. The fund charges you 2.74% of annual fees including the interest expense.

When you see something like that, you have to ask yourself whether a CEF is worth that kind of total expense ratio. We will get to our answer in the performance section, but the title might give away where we are leaning.

Holdings

The top holdings are mainly the usual suspects. Welltower Inc. (WELL), Prologis, Inc. (PLD), Realty Income Corporation (O) and Ventas, Inc. (VTR) are stocks we have written on previously. For a fund having "global" in the title, you sure as heck don't find a lot of non-US holdings in the top 10. You do find one at the tenth position.

The CEF has about two-thirds of its assets in the US and, shockingly, not a single REIT from Canada.

We mention Canada as we have lately found a lot of attractive REITs here and to give you one example where you won't be competing with our subscribers anymore (as the stock has gone up), see this. But we won't hold the lack of Canadian exposure against this fund as we have plenty of other reasons.

Performance

We will examine this in two parts. First, the simple chart from CEF Connect showing performance over different timeframes. Investors must always differentiate between NAV returns and Price returns. NAV returns show you the underlying quality of the fund. Price is dictated by NAV returns of course. But it is influenced by how the discount and premium changes over time.

One notable thing for AWP is that over longer timeframes, price performance is better than NAV performance.

This is what is confusing and fooling the masses. Of course, those are annualized returns so they are even harder to visualize and wrap your head around the compounded impact. But we will help out here. Look at the 5-year total returns on NAV and price below.

We hope people get what a huge impact this change in premium discount has been. Getting into the specifics, again, over 5 years, AWP has swung from a 16% plus discount to a 3% premium.

This is, of course, the biggest trick being pulled on investors focusing on price. You think AWP has legs and can perform. There is a further nuance to this, and that is that all these total return charts assume you are reinvesting the distribution. So you get an extra benefit here of theoretical reinvestments being made at a discount to NAV and then those shares also appreciate towards NAV over time.

But let's not lose sight of the big picture here. The fund has delivered sub 2% compounded returns since inception.

Even if you take the NAV returns from the COVID-19 bottom in March 2020, the total returns on NAV which include distributions, have been horrendous.

We have shown Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (VNQ) above, a fund we are not really fond of, to show just how bad things have been for AWP.

Outlook & Verdict

AWP has had the best of all worlds. Rising stock market, rising REITs, the weak USD (which increases the value of overseas REITs), and, of course, an improving premium. Despite that, it has struggled to really perform. Now imagine a world where some or all of this reverses. Imagine if you go from a 3% premium to a 20% discount and it happens in the context of a bear market.

These are very probable events for a fund that has delivered sub 2% total returns since inception. If you take returns since Inception (the movie, July 16, 2010), AWP still shows you why it should be avoided.

Currently, the risks are extraordinarily high for this 12% yielding fund. We know the underlying stocks barely generate 2%-3% net yield after that 2.74% management expense ratio. AWP relies on capital appreciation and because it has failed to generate it, NAV keeps dropping year after year. More importantly, AWP has to sell stocks every single month to fund the distribution. That results in selling more shares when prices are low than when prices are high. So it follows the reverse of the dollar cost averaging strategy, which improves returns for investors. We rate this a Strong Sell.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Small-Caps Finally Make New High
data.description:
Yesterday, the small-cap Russell 2,000 finally managed to close at a new all-time high when it broke above prior peaks made in December 2024 and December 2021. The last time the index had a breakout after a long period without one was in late 2020 following the COVID Crash. Back then, the index made a huge leg higher after the breakout. Small-cap bulls would love to see a repeat.

Yesterday also marked the end of a 967-trading day streak without a new all-time high for the Russell 2,000. It was the second-longest streak without a new all-time high in the index's history behind the 1,177 day streak that ended in November 2004. The only other comparable streak was the 954 trading days it went without a new high that ended on April 27th, 2011.

Below is a log chart of the Russell with red dots marking the end of the two prior long streaks without new all-time highs mentioned above. Following the 2004 streak, the index continued to chug along higher, but the index saw a pretty nasty sell-off in the months following the end of the 2011 streak.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Corporates Are Leading The Bond Market This Year
data.description:
In a turbulent year for the fixed-income market, corporate bonds have emerged as the top performer for the asset class in 2025, based on a set of ETFs through Thursday’s close (Sep. 18).

The Vanguard Intermediate-Term Corp Bond ETF (VCIT) is leading the field with an 8.3% total return year-to-date. Its long-term corporate counterpart (VCLT) is a close second-place performer this year via an 8.2% gain. Both funds are enjoying a solid lead over the US investment-grade bond benchmark via the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index (BND), which is ahead by 6.2%.

The leadership in corporate bonds has unfolded in a generally bullish environment for fixed income in 2025. Despite a roller coaster of news that has roiled the market at times, all the major bond sectors are posting gains this year. The weakest performer year-to-date: the iShares Short-Maturity Treasury ETF (SHV), a cash proxy, which is up 3.1%.

If the Federal Reserve’s reduction in its target rate announced this week marks the start of a new rate-cutting cycle, the bond market may be poised to extend its rally in the months ahead. Fed funds futures are pricing in a rate cut for each of the remaining two Fed meetings scheduled for October and December.

The question is whether the incoming inflation data will cooperate? Although the Fed is prioritizing the slowdown in the labor market as the bigger concern—thus the decision to cut rates on Wednesday—inflation is still running well above the central bank’s 2% inflation target.

The Consumer Price Index rose 2.9% in August vs. the year-ago level, marking the fourth straight month of accelerating inflation and the highest pace since January. If inflation continues to increase, will the Fed continue to cut rates? Or would hotter inflation force the central bank to pivot to a hawkish stance to contain pricing pressure?

Hanging in the balance is the bond market’s rally this year.

Current expectations by Fed officials suggest a bullish tailwind will continue to blow. Most of the central bank’s policymakers expect interest rates will decline further from current levels.

A number of analysts note that while inflation is edging higher, the increase isn’t sharp or fast enough to warrant rate hikes, or even leaving rates steady. By contrast, the labor market’s slowdown is said to be a more pressing concern, and one that the Fed can address with a degree of efficacy.

“Unless you think you’re really dealing with a runaway inflation, you’re not going to risk a recession,” said Diane Swonk, chief US economist for the accounting firm KPMG.

Considering the Fed’s guidance in its latest expectations for lower rates in the near term, “it would be very unlikely that the Fed did not cut rates at both the upcoming October and December FOMC meetings,” predicts Tim Duy, Chief US Economist, SGH Macro Advisors, in a note sent to clients on Thursday.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Data Centers And The Power Grid: A Path To Debt Relief?
data.description:
Could data centers and the power grid be America’s next “renaissance?” With the U.S. national debt exceeding $37 trillion and interest payments surpassing defense spending, many articles have been written about the “debt doomsday” event coming. Such was a point we made in “The Debt and Deficit Problem.”

In recent months, much debate has been about rising debt and increasing deficit levels in the U.S. For example, here is a recent headline from CNBC:

The article’s author suggests that U.S. federal deficits are ballooning, with spending surging due to the combined impact of tax cuts, expansive stimulus, and entitlement expenditures. Of course, with institutions like Yale, Wharton, and the CBO warning that this trend has pushed interest costs to new heights, now exceeding defense outlays, concerns about domestic solvency are rising. Even prominent figures in the media, from Larry Summers to Ray Dalio, argue that drastic action is urgently needed, otherwise another “financial crisis” is imminent.

As we discussed in that article, the “purveyors of doom” have been saying the same thing for the last two decades, yet the American growth engine continues chugging along. Notably, Ray Dalio and Larry Summers focus on only one solution: “cutting spending,” which has horrible economic consequences.

Furthermore, investors must understand a critical accounting concept: that the government’s debt is the household’s asset. In accounting, for every debit there is a credit that must always equal zero. In this case, when the Government issues debt (a debit), it is sent into the economy for infrastructure, defense, social welfare, etc. That money is “credited” to the bank accounts of households and corporations. Therefore, when the deficit increases, that money winds up in economic activity, and vice versa. In other words, those shouting for sharp deficit reductions are also rooting for a deep economic recession. – The Deficit Narrative

The other challenges with cutting spending are that it is politically toxic, and tax hikes drag on growth.

However, one solution that all the mainstream “doomsayers” overlook is raising productivity and GDP through private sector capital investment. In other words, as the U.S. did following World War II, it is possible to “grow your way out of your debt problem.”

That’s where the AI data center boom and massive electricity demand come in.

The Economic Engine of Capex

The buildout of data centers and the power grid may offer the best opportunity to generate sustained growth. The scale of investment is large enough to matter, the economic multipliers are high, and the timeline aligns with when fiscal pressure will peak.

Data centers are the backbone of the digital economy. AI models, cloud platforms, and automation rely on them, and each large AI model requires thousands of GPUs housed in purpose-built facilities. These are not minor server rooms. They are $1 billion-plus industrial complexes. But, for the build-out of “data factories” to impact economic growth, a massive investment will be required to affect a $30 trillion (nominal) U.S. economy.

How much are we talking about? As noted in our previous article on data center demand, McKinsey projects that data center investment will reach $7 trillion globally by 2030. Over 40 percent of that will happen in the U.S. alone. Moreover, Nvidia’s latest quarterly report forecasted data center capital expenditures by Apple, Meta, Microsoft, and Alphabet to reach $4 trillion by 2030, or roughly $1 trillion annually.

However, you can’t build a data center without power.

Data Centers Require Power

According to the Department of Energy, U.S. data center electricity consumption could double or triple by 2028. Deloitte expects a 30-fold increase in AI-related data center power usage by 2035, reaching 123 gigawatts. That’s more than the total residential demand in California. This means building new substations, transmission lines, and power plants. Therefore, as noted by Edison Electric Institute, investor-owned U.S. electric utilities will invest more than $1.1 trillion in the 2025-2029 period to meet that demand.

This infrastructure boom will not be optional. AI development needs low latency, which requires local capacity and reliable electricity. Since data centers are capital-intensive and mission-critical, the U.S. economy could see between $1.25 and $1.5 trillion in annual capex spending.

Why is that critical to our “debt-to-GDP” concerns? Large-scale capital spending is one of the few economic inputs reliably boosting long-term growth. Unlike stimulus checks or tax credits, infrastructure spending creates durable productivity improvements. We are already seeing this impact.

  • The PwC study for the Data Center Coalition found that from 2017 to 2021, U.S. data centers added $2.1 trillion to GDP. That’s before the AI acceleration began.
  • According to Reuters, in Q2 2025, AI-related capex accounted for over one-third of real GDP growth, which added roughly 0.4 percentage points to quarterly GDP.

The Multiplier Effect

But it isn’t just $1 trillion in CapEx. These projects create upstream demand for construction, power equipment, semiconductors, cooling systems, and skilled labor. They support engineering, real estate, logistics, and utility services. Most notably, they set the stage for sustained gains in productivity. Once operational, AI systems reduce costs, improve decision-making, and enable scale in industries from health care to manufacturing. In other words, there is a “multiplier effect” to these projects. The American Society of Civil Engineers [ASCE] estimates that every $1 billion in infrastructure investment creates 13,000 jobs and adds $3 billion to GDP over a decade.

Therefore, by 2030, if the $5 trillion in combined investment in AI data centers and power generation comes to fruition, the U.S. economy will see an additional $15 trillion in growth. The chart below shows the average 5-year growth rate in nominal GDP and projected growth.

The chart below assumes we will continue to issue debt at the average quarterly pace since 2021. However, instead of wasting money, we focus on productive investments while maintaining all current spending programs and obligations. Assuming some conservative growth estimates resulting from the investments, the “debt to GDP” ratio will begin declining in 2026 and revert to more sustainable levels by 2030.

However, this isn’t a guarantee, but it is certainly potentially more realistic than a “debt crisis” that sends the U.S. into an economic depression.

Risks and Constraints

The growth opportunity is significant, but not risk-free. While the bears constantly ring the alarm bell about the current level of debt and deficits, the more dire economic consequences they forecast may fail to come to fruition.

As noted by Goldman Sachs:

Generative artificial intelligence has the potential to automate many work tasks and eventually boost global economic growth. AI will start having a measurable impact on US GDP in 2027 and begin affecting growth in other economies worldwide in the following years. The foundation of the forecast is the finding that AI could ultimately automate around 25% of labor tasks in advanced economies and 10-20% of work in emerging economies.

They currently estimate a growth boost to GDP from AI of 0.4 percentage points in the US.

Increases in productivity, productive capital investment, and increased labor demand for the infrastructure buildout (which will also result in higher wages) should provide the economic boost needed.

Conclusion

Will it solve all of the current socio-economic ills facing the U.S.? No. However, it may provide the growth boost necessary to revitalize economic growth and prosperity in the U.S., which we have not seen since the 1970s. But there are risks to this outlook.

  1. The scale of spending may lift electricity prices. A study from NC State found national prices could rise 8 percent by 2030, with some localities seeing 20–25 percent jumps. If not managed, this could erode industrial competitiveness.
  2. Jobs per dollar of investment are low. Data centers are automated. Once built, they require few workers. A $1 billion facility may employ only 100 people full-time.
  3. There is a local backlash. Projects require land, water, and energy. Communities are resisting tax incentives and environmental costs. Several counties have imposed moratoriums.
  4. Permitting delays and regulatory hurdles remain high. The average interconnection wait for new power projects is over 3 years. Without reform, many AI investments will be delayed or redirected.

The government will need to be involved to unlock the full economic benefit.

  1. Public policy must streamline permitting, including environmental review, grid interconnection, and land use.
  2. Given that infrastructure and national security are at stake, a public/private partnership should be involved.
  3. Tax policy should avoid distortion. Many subsidies go to projects that were already economically viable.
  4. The focus should be on shared infrastructure, such as transmission lines, regional data hubs, and reliability upgrades.
  5. Utilities need regulatory clarity. Traditional cost-of-service models may not align with the fast pace of AI demand, so performance-based rates or return-on-equity mechanisms may be required.
  6. Energy policy must support generation diversity. Solar and wind are inefficient, and AI demands require firm natural gas, nuclear, and advanced storage capacity.

The goal should be energy abundance. High reliability, low prices, and scalable infrastructure will attract private AI investment and increase growth.

It may also just be enough to keep the demise of the U.S. from occurring any time soon.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Small/Mid Cap Equity Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The S&P 500 Index increased by 10.94% (total return, in USD) in the second quarter of 2025, whereas the Russell 2000 Index increased by 8.50% (total return, in USD). Despite an initial selloff prompted by concern regarding new US tariff policies, the market surged this quarter, supported by progress in trade negotiations, a resilient labor market, strong corporate earnings results, and better-than-expected inflationary data. Specifically, easing trade tensions and renewed investor optimism toward the artificial intelligence growth theme served as key tailwinds, contributing to the S&P 500 Index reaching all-time highs during the period. Furthermore, a series of better-than-expected Core Consumer Price Index reports, combined with resilient consumer spending trends, underpinned the strength of the US economy. The best performing sectors within the S&P 500 were Information Technology, Communication Services, and Industrials, while the worst performing sectors were Energy, Health Care, and Real Estate. For the Russell 2000, the best performing sectors were Information Technology, Industrials, and Materials, while the worst performing sectors were Real Estate, Utilities, and Consumer Staples.

*Effective after the close of business on June 20, 2025, the Goldman Sachs Small/Mid Cap Value Fund changed its name to the Goldman Sachs Small/Mid Cap Equity Fund, changed its performance benchmark from the Russell 2500 Value® Index to the Russell 2500® Index, and certain of its principal investment strategies. Performance information prior to this date reflects the Fund's former strategies. For periods one year or greater, performance is annualized. The returns represent past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. The Fund’s investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted above. Please visit am.gs.com to obtain the most recent month-end returns. Performance reflects cumulative total returns for periods of less than one year and average annual total returns for periods of greater than one year. All Fund performance data reflect the reinvestment of distributions.

Portfolio Attribution

The Goldman Sachs Small/Mid Cap Equity Fund (GSMVX) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Index (net), during the quarter. Stock selection in the Industrials and Energy sectors contributed the most to relative returns, while stock selection within Financials and Consumer Discretionary detracted from relative returns.

Curtiss-Wright Corporation (CW)(1.7%), a provider of highly engineered products and services to the aerospace, defense, and industrial markets, was the top contributor to relative returns during the period. The company’s stock thrived from continued growth in their order book, a strong backlog, and robust earnings beats. We like Curtiss-Wright’s diversified end-market exposure to various growth markets, unrivaled execution on their commitments, and strong balance sheet to deploy capital.

MasTec, Inc. (MTZ)(1.2%), an infrastructure engineering and construction company, was another top contributor to relative returns during the quarter. MasTec’s stock appreciation was fueled by a beat and raise in its first quarter earnings report, highlighted by backlog strength and continued improving margins. We like MasTec as it gives us exposure to several infrastructure buildout-related end markets like communications, transmission and distribution, solar and wind power plants, etc. at compelling valuations. We also like that MasTec has deleveraged its balance sheet relative to last year.

The Standardized Total Returns are average annual total returns or cumulative total returns (only if the performance period is one year or less) as of the most recent calendar quarter end. They assume reinvestment of all distributions at net asset value. Class A shares reflect the maximum initial sales charge of 5.50%. Because Institutional shares do not include a sales charge, such a charge is not included in the standardized total returns.

Expense Ratios

The expense ratios of the Fund, both current (net of any fee waivers or expense limitations) and before waivers (gross of any fee waivers or expense limitations) are as set forth above. Pursuant to a contractual arrangement, the Fund’s waivers and/or expense limitations will remain in place through at least December 29, 2025, and prior to such date the investment adviser may not terminate the arrangements without the approval of the Fund’s Board of Trustees. Please refer to the Fund’s prospectus for the most recent expenses.

Guidewire Software Inc. (GWRE)(1.8%), a vertical software company serving the property and casualty (P&C) insurance industry, was the top detractor from relative returns during the quarter due to our mistimed entry into the position. We purchased the stock shortly after its strong rally – following Guidewire’s excellent earnings report – and the stock proceeded to pull back towards the end of the quarter. We have confidence in Guidewire as the company is one of the largest players in the vertical focused software space, which tends to have less competition, stickier customers, and steadier businesses. Furthermore, the migration to cloud has reached a tipping point for insurers, and Guidewire is the cloud leader.

Globus Medical Inc. (GMED)(1.0%), a company that designs and commercializes medical devices aimed at improving outcomes for patients with musculoskeletal conditions, was another top detractor from relative returns during the quarter. Globus Medical's stock fell sharply after a disappointing first-quarter earnings report, which showed a decline in sales. The company faced several headwinds including integration-related supply chain issues, delayed Enabling Technology (ET) deals, and international distributor order timing. Despite the disappointing quarter, we believe the headwinds were temporary, and we have confidence in Globus Medical as a market share gainer driven by unrivaled innovation. In our view, the company is well positioned for further margin expansion driven by a pull forward in cost savings.

Portfolio Review

We initiated a position in RBC Bearings Incorporated (RBC)(2.0%), a company that designs, manufactures, and markets bearing products, during the quarter. We like RBC due to their continued strength in gross margins, solid backlog momentum, and healthy balance sheet. We also believe renewed contracts with Boeing/Airbus with better pricing in January 2026 should be additive to margins

We also initiated a position in Guidewire Software, Inc. (1.8%), a vertical software company serving the property and casualty (P&C) insurance industry. Vertical focused software tends to have less competition, stickier customers, and steadier businesses, and GuideWire is one of the largest players in the space and continues to take share. Furthermore, the migration to cloud has reached a tipping point for insurers, and Guidewire is the cloud leader.

We exited our position in TXNM Energy, Inc. (TXNM)(0.0%), an energy holding company that generates, transmits, and distributes electricity to customers in New Mexico and Texas, during the quarter. We ultimately decided to liquidate the position and reallocate capital to other companies where we had higher conviction in their growth trajectories and risk-reward profiles.

Fund holdings and allocations may not include the Fund’s entire investment portfolio, which may change at any time. Fund holdings should not be relied on in making investment decisions and should not be construed as research or investment advice regarding particular securities. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.

We also exited our position in bank holding company, Webster Financial Corporation (WBS)(0.0%), during the quarter. We ultimately exited the position in favor of higher conviction ideas elsewhere in the portfolio.

Strategy/Outlook

The second quarter was characterized by themes of resilience as the US equity market recovered and outperformed from the previous quarter’s weakness, as strong quarterly earnings and the improvement of newly implemented trade policy positioning led to a resurgence in investor optimism. As we traverse ever-changing market conditions, we believe active management is critical to navigating economic uncertainties, in pursuit of providing diversified sources of returns. Unlike passive management strategies, which may carry exposure to low-quality constituents, taking an active approach may enable investors to avoid potential pitfalls and adapt to shifting macroeconomic conditions and geopolitical backdrop. As we navigate this period of heightened volatility, we remain nimble and look to capitalize on idiosyncratic opportunities uncovered through bottom-up stock selection. We continue to prioritize our quality-oriented approach to investing by focusing on having a long- term viewpoint on the portfolio, seeking businesses with healthy balance sheets, and partnering with management teams that are effective stewards of capital. In our view, we are optimistic that a fundamental approach may generate excess returns in the long run for our clients.

Fund holdings should not be relied on in making investment decisions and should not be construed as research or investment advice regarding particular securities. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.

Risk Considerations

The Goldman Sachs Small/Mid Cap Equity Fund invests primarily in mid- and small-capitalization U.S. equity investments. The Fund's investments are subject to market risk, which means that the value of the securities in which it invests may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies, particular sectors or governments and/or general economic conditions. The securities of mid- and small-capitalization companies involve greater risks than those associated with larger, more established companies and may be subject to more abrupt or erratic price movements. Foreign and emerging markets investments may be more volatile and less liquid than investments in U.S. securities and are subject to the risks of currency fluctuations and adverse economic, social or political developments. Different investment styles (e.g., "quantitative") tend to shift in and out of favor, and at times the Fund may underperform other funds that invest in similar asset classes. Investing in REITs involves certain unique risks in addition to those risks associated with investing in the real estate industry in general. REITs whose underlying properties are focused in a particular industry or geographic region are also subject to risks affecting such industries and regions. The securities of REITs involve greater risks than those associated with larger, more established companies and may be subject to more abrupt or erratic price movements because of interest rate changes, economic conditions and other factors. The Fund may have a high rate of portfolio turnover, which involves correspondingly greater expenses which must be borne by the Fund, and is also likely to result in short-term capital gains taxable to shareholders.

General Disclosures

The S&P 500 Index is the Standard & Poor’s 500 Composite Stock Prices Index of 500 stocks, an unmanaged index of common stock prices.

Russell 2500 Index measures the performance of the 2,500 smallest companies in the Russell 3000 Index.

The Russell 2000® index is an index measuring the performance of approximately 2,000 small-cap companies in the Russell 3000 Index, which is made up of 3,000 of the biggest U.S. stocks. The Russell 2000 serves as a benchmark for small-cap stocks in the United States. Investors cannot invest directly in indices.
